Hint: Initial part of the session when the market tests key levels and quickly reveals balance or directional intent.
Main body of the session after the open, where the market either continues, forms a range, or transitions.
Final part of the session when closing flows increase and price is often drawn toward key magnets or day extremes.
Tight Trading Range; many overlapping bars in a small range, showing balance and low urgency.
Trading Range; two-sided rotation between support and resistance with frequent reversals and failed breakouts.
Trend that advances in steps—impulse, pause, shallow pullback, then continuation.
Strong channel where pullbacks stay separated from the moving average or prior breakout area, showing persistent pressure.
Directional market with strong closes, shallow pullbacks, and repeated continuation pressure.
Three pushes in the same direction with weakening momentum, often leading to a pullback or reversal.
Double Bottom/Top; a second test of a prior extreme that can lead to reversal or range continuation.
An unusually large breakout bar followed by confirming bars, showing a sudden and sustained imbalance.
A large breakout bar that is not confirmed by the next bars, often signaling exhaustion or a trap.
Accelerated move with one or more unusually strong late bars, often followed by profit-taking, pause, or reversal.
Small pause late in a mature trend that can produce one last breakout before the trend ends.
The second swing in a pullback or trend resumption after the first leg and intervening pause or pullback.
Breakout that is confirmed by immediate continuation, increasing the odds the move is genuine.
Breakout, pullback test, then a second-entry signal in the breakout direction.
Inside-bar breakout-mode patterns showing contraction and the potential for expansion in either direction.
Broadening formation with higher highs and lower lows, reflecting growing volatility and two-sided disagreement.
Contracting range with lower highs and higher lows, reflecting compression before a breakout attempt.
Ten Bars, Two Legs correction; a deeper pullback that typically unfolds over about 10 bars and at least two legs.
After about 20 or more sideways bars, the market is close to neutral and breakout odds become more balanced.
Reversal structure where a prior trend gives way to a new trend through a higher high/higher low or lower low/lower high sequence.
Small structural reversal that changes the immediate swing but usually stays within a broader trading range rather than starting a strong new trend.
Psychologically important price level ending in a clean increment that often acts as support, resistance, or a magnet.
Retracement of roughly half of a prior swing, often watched as a common support or resistance test.
Signal bar closing near its high, showing buying pressure and bullish intent.
Signal bar closing near its midpoint, showing balanced pressure and weaker directional conviction.
Signal bar closing near its low, showing selling pressure and bearish intent.
Bar with a prominent tail rejecting a price area, often used as reversal evidence when context supports it.
Bar closing on or near its extreme with little opposite-side wick, showing urgency and breakout strength.
Small-bodied bar showing indecision and balance, usually needing stronger context or confirmation.
Short hold and quick target logic.
Hold through larger structure with wider thesis.
Keep a residual position for extension after partial profit.
Plan to add size only on predefined confirmation.
Plan staged exits into target/structure.
